cards.text: The three logical operators are: {{c1::!}} (NOT), {{c2::&&}} (AND), and {{c3::||}} (OR).

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: AND operator returns true only if both operands are true. true && false = false (printed as 0).

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: OR operator returns true if at least one operand is true. true || false = true (printed as 1).

cards.text: The logical NOT operator {{c1::!}} is {{c2::unary}} (takes one operand). !true = {{c3::false}} and !false = {{c4::true}}.

cards.back: When using && or ||, if the result can be determined from the left operand alone, the right operand is not evaluated. For &&, if left is false, right isn't evaluated. For ||, if left is true, right isn't evaluated.

cards.question: Will printHello() be called?
cards.explanation: Short-circuit evaluation: false && anything is always false, so printHello() is never called.

cards.text: De Morgan's laws: !(a && b) is equivalent to {{c1::!a || !b}}, and !(a || b) is equivalent to {{c2::!a && !b}}.

cards.front: What does the logical AND truth table look like?
cards.back:
true && true = true
true && false = false
false && true = false
false && false = false
(Only true when both are true)

cards.front: What does the logical OR truth table look like?
cards.back:
true || true = true
true || false = true
false || true = true
false || false = false
(Only false when both are false)

cards.question: What does this check?
cards.answer: Whether x is in the range [1, 10] inclusive
cards.explanation: Checks if x is greater than or equal to 1 AND less than or equal to 10. Both conditions must be true.

cards.text: The logical operators have {{c1::lower}} precedence than relational operators, so x > 5 && x < 10 is evaluated as {{c2::(x > 5) && (x < 10)}}.

cards.explanation: Short-circuit evaluation can be used to avoid expensive operations or prevent errors. Place cheaper or more likely-to-fail conditions first in && expressions, and more likely-to-succeed conditions first in || expressions.
examples: if (ptr != nullptr && *ptr == 5)  // Safe: nullptr check first

cards.front: What is the alternative keyword for the logical NOT operator?
cards.back: The keyword 'not' can be used instead of !. So !x and 'not x' are equivalent. Similarly, 'and' for && and 'or' for ||.

cards.text: Logical operators: {{c1::!}} (NOT) flips the value, {{c2::&&}} (AND) requires both to be true, {{c3::||}} (OR) requires at least one to be true.
With {{c1::short-circuit}} evaluation, the {{c2::right}} operand is not evaluated if the result can be determined from the {{c3::left}} operand alone.
